_metadata.description: KisanMind crop knowledge base. Covers major Indian crops with sowing windows, water needs, soil compatibility, and risk factors by region.

units.water_need_mm: total millimetres of water required across full growing cycle
units.irrigation_interval_days: days between irrigation events under normal conditions

wheat.name: Wheat
wheat.description: Primary winter staple crop. Requires cool temperatures during germination and grain filling. Highly sensitive to late sowing — yield drops sharply after optimal window.

north_india.notes: Punjab, Haryana, UP belt. Ideal temp at sowing: 20–22°C. Late sowing (post Nov 25) reduces yield by 30–40 kg/hectare per day of delay.

central_india.notes: MP, Rajasthan. Slightly later window due to slower temperature drop.

critical_stages.note: Missing irrigation at this stage causes 20–25% yield loss
High water demand phase begins
Water stress here causes grain shrivelling
Final critical stage — maintain adequate moisture

soil.notes: Well-drained deep soils essential. Black cotton soil retains moisture well for wheat but needs drainage management.

risk_factors.message: Heat stress during grain filling causes yield loss of up to 50%
Rain at harvest causes grain discolouration and quality loss
Late sowing penalty: 30–40 kg/ha yield loss per day of delay
Yellow rust risk increases when humidity exceeds 80% during vegetative stage

market.notes: MSP-backed crop. Price predictable. Sell at harvest or store for April–May premium.

rice.name: Rice
rice.description: Primary kharif staple. High water consumer. Benefits from monsoon timing. Multiple varieties — short duration (90 days) to long duration (150 days).

north_india.notes: Transplanting typically 25–30 days after nursery sowing. Punjab: delay transplanting past July 10 to reduce groundwater depletion.

south_india.notes: Dependent on southwest monsoon onset. Second crop possible Oct–Feb.

east_india.notes: WB, Odisha, Bihar. Flash flooding risk in low-lying areas — avoid if topography indicates flood susceptibility.

critical_stages.note: Maintain 5cm standing water for 15 days post-transplant
Alternate wetting and drying saves 30% water with <5% yield loss
Critical — water stress here causes sterility
Maintain 2–5cm water during entire flowering period

soil.notes: Heavy soils that retain water are ideal. Sandy soils cause excessive percolation losses.

risk_factors.message: Water stress at flowering causes panicle sterility — yield loss up to 70%
Cold temperatures cause spikelet sterility in most varieties
BPH risk high when humidity exceeds 85% for 5+ consecutive days
Pre-harvest rainfall causes grain sprouting and quality loss

market.notes: MSP-backed. Basmati commands significant premium — check variety before advising on market timing.

cotton.name: Cotton
cotton.description: High-value cash crop. Requires long frost-free season. Bt cotton dominates in India. Sensitive to waterlogging.

west_india.notes: Gujarat, Maharashtra. Pre-monsoon sowing (May 15–June 1) with irrigation gives best results.

central_india.notes: MP, Vidarbha. Rain-fed possible if monsoon onset is on time. Waterlogging risk on black soils in heavy rain years.

south_india.notes: AP, Telangana. Two pickings typical. First picking accounts for 60–70% of yield.

critical_stages.note: Ensure adequate moisture — dry spell after sowing causes poor stand
Bud formation — water stress causes bud shedding
Highest water demand period — 14-day irrigation cycle
Reduce irrigation to promote uniform boll opening

soil.notes: Black cotton (Vertisol) is ideal — deep, moisture retentive. Avoid fields with waterlogging history.

risk_factors.message: Cotton cannot tolerate standing water — even 48 hours causes root damage
Pink bollworm is the primary yield threat — scouting essential from squaring stage
Rainfall during harvest period stains lint — market price penalty
Late kharif sowing reduces boll count and harvest extends into cold season

market.notes: Global cotton prices affect domestic. Check MCX futures before deciding on immediate vs stored sale.

maize.name: Maize
maize.description: Versatile crop grown as kharif staple and rabi irrigation crop. Shorter cycle than rice. Good drought tolerance relative to other cereals.

north_india.notes: HP, Uttarakhand hills: May–June sowing. Plains: June–July with monsoon. Rabi maize possible in South and East India.

south_india.notes: Rabi crop also viable (Nov–Feb) under irrigation. Karnataka is a major producer.

critical_stages.note: Rapid growth phase — maintain soil moisture
Most critical — water stress here causes poor pollination and blank cobs
Coincides with tasseling — combined stress period is highest risk
Maintain moisture for kernel development

soil.notes: Well-drained loamy soils ideal. Does not tolerate standing water. Good option for medium-fertility soils.

risk_factors.message: 8–10 day drought at tasseling/silking causes blank cobs — most damaging stress period
Waterlogging in first 30 days causes stunted growth and root rot
Fall armyworm (FAW) is now endemic in India — scout whorls from 2-leaf stage

market.notes: Poultry and starch industry demand drives prices. Feed sector buys at harvest — prices typically rise post-harvest as supply tightens.

tomato.name: Tomato
tomato.description: High-value vegetable with 3 planting seasons possible. Extreme price volatility. Short cycle enables fast cash flow but requires intensive management.

north_india.notes: Spring-summer crop. Also viable as Sep–Oct sowing for Nov–Jan harvest (winter crop). Avoid peak monsoon transplanting.

south_india.notes: Three seasons possible. Kharif (Jul–Aug), Rabi (Nov–Dec), Summer (Feb–Mar). Protected cultivation extends options.

critical_stages.note: Daily light irrigation for 7 days post-transplant for establishment
Irregular irrigation causes blossom drop and blossom end rot
Consistent moisture critical — erratic watering causes fruit cracking
Reduce water 1 week before harvest to improve shelf life

soil.notes: Well-drained light soils best. Heavy clay causes root diseases. Raised beds recommended in high-rainfall areas.

risk_factors.message: Pollen sterility above 38°C causes complete flower drop — avoid summer sowing in hot regions
Early blight and late blight risk increases sharply above 80% humidity
Tomato prices crash during peak harvest windows — stagger sowing dates if possible
Cracking and rotting of mature fruit during rainfall — harvest immediately if rain forecast

market.notes: Most volatile vegetable crop. Prices can swing 10x within a season. Target Nov–Jan window when supply from plains is lowest. Consider FPO aggregation for better price realization.

soybean.name: Soybean
soybean.description: Major oilseed and protein crop. Central India's primary kharif cash crop. Nitrogen-fixing legume — improves soil health. Sensitive to photoperiod.

central_india.notes: MP and Vidarbha are primary belts. Sow with first good monsoon rain (soil moisture > 50mm). Avoid sowing in dry soil — poor germination.

west_india.notes: Rajasthan, Gujarat. Shorter season varieties preferred. Drought risk in low-rainfall years.

critical_stages.note: Soil moisture > 50% field capacity needed for uniform germination
Most sensitive stage — flower drop under moisture stress
Seed size determined here — adequate moisture increases oil content
Dry conditions preferred for clean harvest and reduced mould

soil.notes: Deep black soils of MP and Maharashtra are ideal. pH below 6.0 reduces nitrogen fixation efficiency. Rhizobium seed treatment recommended.

risk_factors.message: Waterlogging for 48+ hours in early stage causes root rot and stand loss
Moisture stress at pod filling reduces seed size and oil content by 15–20%
Girdle beetle is endemic in central India — girdling causes stem breakage
Soybean is photoperiod-sensitive — late sowing shortens vegetative period and reduces yield

market.notes: Prices linked to global edible oil market (palm oil, soyoil). Post-harvest prices typically lower — consider farm storage for 60–90 days.
